title: Contact dermatitis
license_notes: Use as public patient-education source support. Avoid verbatim copying into primitives unless reuse terms are confirmed for the exact content.
supports: Contact dermatitis is skin redness, soreness, or inflammation after direct contact with a substance.
Contact dermatitis can be irritant or allergic, and common triggers include soaps, detergents, cosmetics, plants, adhesives, metals, latex, and topical medicines.
Evaluation is based on the appearance and exposure history, and patch testing may be needed for recurrent or persistent cases.
Treatment depends on the cause and may include avoiding the trigger, moisturizers, topical corticosteroids, wet dressings, or clinician-directed medicines.
Patients should seek care for severe reactions, lack of improvement, or signs of infection such as tenderness, redness, warmth, or fever.
